planting.note: Plant container or balled stock in spring in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; silky dogwood grows naturally in streamside thickets, wet meadows, and pond margins and is one of the best native shrubs for stabilizing stream banks and rain gardens.

directions: Plant container or balled stock in spring in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; silky dogwood grows naturally in streamside thickets, wet meadows, and pond margins and is one of the best native shrubs for stabilizing stream banks and rain gardens. Cornus amomum is a loosely branched, multi-stemmed native shrub with soft, somewhat silky-haired leaves, flat-topped clusters of small white flowers in June that are outstanding for native bees and small butterflies, and blue-black berry-like drupes in fall that are among the most heavily consumed native fruits by migrating songbirds — particularly wood thrushes, robins, and waxwings; the red-purple winter stems provide dormant season interest. Plant in groupings for best berry production and wildlife impact.

planting.note: Plant container or field-grown stock in spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich soil in partial shade to full sun; pagoda dogwood is shade-tolerant and adaptable but is susceptible to dogwood borers and golden canker disease — provide good air circulation and avoid injuring the bark.

directions: Plant container or field-grown stock in spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich soil in partial shade to full sun; pagoda dogwood is shade-tolerant and adaptable but is susceptible to dogwood borers and golden canker disease — provide good air circulation and avoid injuring the bark. Cornus alternifolia is the only native eastern dogwood with alternate (rather than opposite) leaves, and it produces one of the most distinctive and elegant branching patterns of any native tree — distinctly horizontal, tiered layers of branches that create a pagoda-like architectural silhouette outstanding in winter; its small, fragrant, flat-topped white flower clusters are followed by showy blue-black berries on red pedicels that are consumed by dozens of migrating bird species. Four-season interest: layered winter form, white spring flowers, summer fruit, and red-purple fall foliage.

planting.note: Plant container or bare-root stock in spring or fall in virtually any soil — moist or dry, acidic or neutral, clay or sand — in partial shade to full sun; black chokeberry is one of the most adaptable native shrubs in North America, thriving under conditions that defeat most other fruiting shrubs.

directions: Plant container or bare-root stock in spring or fall in virtually any soil — moist or dry, acidic or neutral, clay or sand — in partial shade to full sun; black chokeberry is one of the most adaptable native shrubs in North America, thriving under conditions that defeat most other fruiting shrubs. Aronia melanocarpa produces stunning clusters of small white flowers with prominent pink anthers in May, glossy, dark-green summer foliage, and massive clusters of small, dark-purple to near-black berries in August and September that persist into winter; the berries are extremely high in anthocyanins and antioxidants — among the highest recorded for any fruit — with an astringent, tart flavor excellent for juices, syrups, and wine. Fall foliage is a brilliant scarlet-red. The plant is essentially bulletproof once established, tolerating wet soils, clay, drought, and partial shade; it spreads by root suckers into dense thickets over time.

planting.note: Plant container or field-grown stock in spring in moist to dry, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained to moderately moist soil in partial shade to full sun; nannyberry is one of the most shade-tolerant native viburnums and grows naturally as a large shrub or small tree at the edges of moist woodlands and thickets.

directions: Plant container or field-grown stock in spring in moist to dry, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained to moderately moist soil in partial shade to full sun; nannyberry is one of the most shade-tolerant native viburnums and grows naturally as a large shrub or small tree at the edges of moist woodlands and thickets. Viburnum lentago is a large native viburnum of the northeastern and central United States, producing shiny, deeply veined, finely toothed leaves and flat-topped clusters of creamy-white flowers in late spring that are outstanding for native bees; the abundant, blue-black, berry-like drupes in fall and winter provide critical food for migrating and overwintering songbirds — particularly cedar waxwings, robins, and thrushes — and are also edible for humans, with a sweet, date-raisin flavor when fully ripe. The fall foliage turns rich wine-red to purplish-red; the plant often sends up suckers and gradually forms a thicket.

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; viburnum nudum is one of the best viburnums for wet or poorly drained sites and thrives in rain gardens, pond margins, and low areas where standing water occasionally occurs.

directions: Plant container stock in spring in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; viburnum nudum is one of the best viburnums for wet or poorly drained sites and thrives in rain gardens, pond margins, and low areas where standing water occasionally occurs. Viburnum nudum produces one of the most spectacular fall berry displays of any native shrub: the abundant berry clusters transition through pink, rose, blue, and finally blue-black as individual berries ripen at different times in September and October, creating a multi-colored cluster that is breathtaking and irresistible to migrating songbirds; the berries are consumed within days of full ripeness by cedar waxwings, bluebirds, and dozens of other species. The glossy, semi-evergreen to evergreen foliage turns rich burgundy-red in fall; white, fragrant flower clusters in May attract native bees.
